explanation:
Combination reaction - no example given

Decomposition reaction - $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{aq})} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\ell)}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$

$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\ell)} \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} $$

Displacement reaction - $\mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+2 \mathrm{Br}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}+\mathrm{Br}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$

Disproportionation reaction - $2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{aq})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\ell)}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$

content: In the reaction between hydrogen sulphide and acidified permanganate solution,

In the titration of potassium permanganate $\left(\mathrm{KMnO}_4\right)$ against Ferrous ammonium sulphate $(\mathrm{FAS})$ solution, dilute sulphuric acid but not nitric acid is used to maintain acidic medium, because

options.content: It is difficult to identify the end point
Nitric acid doesn't act as an indicator
Nitric acid itself is an oxidizing agent
Nitric acid is a weak acid than sulphuric acid

explanation: $$ \text { Nitric acid is not used in the redox titration because it is a strong } \mathrm{OA} \text { itself. } $$

content: In the reaction between moist $\mathrm{SO}_2$ and acidified permanganate solution.

options.content: $\mathrm{SO}_2$ is reduced to S . $\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}$is oxidised to $\mathrm{MnO}_4$.

explanation:
The reaction between moist $\mathrm{SO}_2$ and acidified permanganate solution is as follows

$$2 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+3 \mathrm{SO}_2+4 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+3 \mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}+\mathrm{2H_2O}$$

Thus, from the reaction it is clear that $\mathrm{SO}_2$ is oxidised to $\mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}$ while $\mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}$is reduced to $\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}$.

content:
The reducing agent in the given equations

$$4 \mathrm{Ag}(s)+8 \mathrm{CN}^{-}(a q)+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(a q)+\mathrm{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow 4\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_2\right]^{-}(a q)+4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q)$$

$$2\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_2\right]^{-}(a q)+\mathrm{Zn}(s) \longrightarrow {\left[\mathrm{Zn}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^{2-}(a q)+2 \mathrm{Ag}(s)}$$
explanation: $$\mathrm{Zn}$$ acts as a reducing agent in the given reactions. It reduces $$\mathrm{Ag}$$ and itself gets oxidised.

$$8 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+3 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{O}_3^{2-}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow 8 \mathrm{MnO}_2+6 \mathrm{SO}_4^{2-}+2 \mathrm{OH}^{-}$$

From the reaction it is clear that, $$a=8$$ and $$y=6$$.

content: In which of the following compounds, an element exhibits two different oxidation states?
explanation: In $$\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_3, \mathrm{~N}$$ exhibits two different oxidation states. Actually $$\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_3$$ exists as $$\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}$$ and $$\mathrm{NO}_3^{-}$$. Oxidation state of $$\mathrm{N}$$ in $$\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}$$ is $$-3$$ and in $$\mathrm{NO}_3^{-}$$ is $$+5$$.

content: All Cu(II) halides are known, except the iodide, the reason for it is that

options.content: $$\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$$ has much more negative hydration enthalpy

explanation:
$$2 \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}+4 \mathrm{I}^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CuI}_2$$

This $$\mathrm{Cu}$$ (II) iodide immediately decomposes to liberate $$\mathrm{I}_2$$ and insoluble copper (I) iodide.

$$2 \mathrm{CuI}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CuI}+\mathrm{I}_2$$

content: Which of the following is not true for oxidation?
explanation: From the given option only (d) option is not true for oxidation i.e. removal of electronegative element does not takes place in case of oxidation.

content: How many moles of acidified $$\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7$$ is required to liberate 6 moles of $$\mathrm{I}_2$$ from an aqueous solution of $$\mathrm{I}^{-}$$?
explanation:
We know,

$$\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{-2}+6 \mathrm{I}^{-}+14 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{I}_2+7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$$

Here, 3 moles of $$\mathrm{I}_2$$ liberation requires one $$\mathrm{mol}$$ of $$\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7$$; for getting 6 moles of $$\mathrm{I}_2$$, we will require two moles of $$\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7$$.

content: The oxidation number of nitrogen atoms in $$\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{NO}_3$$ are
explanation:
$$\mathop {N{H_4}}\limits_{({N_1})} \mathop {N{O_3}}\limits_{({N_2})} \buildrel {} \over \longrightarrow NH_4^ + + NO_3^ - $$

For oxidation number of

$$\begin{array}{r} N_1 \rightarrow x+4=+1 \\ x=+1-4=-3 \end{array}$$

So, oxidation state of first nitrogen is $$-3$$.

For oxidation state of second nitrogen $$\left(N_2\right) \rightarrow$$

$$\begin{aligned} x-6 & =-1 \\ x & =-1+6=+5 \end{aligned}$$

So, the answer is $$-3$$ and $$+5$$.

content: In which of the following cases a chemical reaction is possible?

options.content: $$\mathrm{ZnSO}_4(a q)$$ is placed in a copper vessel
$$\mathrm{AgNO}_3$$ solution is stirred with a copper spoon
Conc. $$\mathrm{HNO}_3$$ is stored in a platinum vessel
gold ornaments are washed with dil. $$\mathrm{HCl}$$

explanation:
A chemical reaction is possible when $$\mathrm{AgNO}_3$$ solution is stirred with copper spoon as copper has lesser positive value of standard electrode reduction potential as compared to $$\mathrm{Ag}$$.

The reaction will be,

$$2 \mathrm{AgNO}_3+\mathrm{Cu} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)_2+2 \mathrm{Ag}$$

content: The number of moles of electron required to reduce 0.2 mole of $$\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{-2}$$ to $$\mathrm{Cr}^{+3}$$ is
explanation:
The number of moles of electrons required to reduce 0.2 mole of $$\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}$$ to $$\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$$ is explained below.

To find this, consider the reduction equation:

$$\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-} + 6 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$$

From this equation, we see that 1 mole of $$\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}$$ requires 6 moles of electrons for the reduction process.

Therefore, for 0.2 mole of $$\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}$$, the number of moles of electrons required can be calculated as:

$$6 \times 0.2 = 1.2 \text{ moles}$$

Thus, 1.2 moles of electrons are needed to reduce 0.2 mole of $$\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}$$ to $$\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$$.

content: Which of the following statement is correct?

options.content: $$\mathrm{Cl}_2$$ is a stronger oxidising agent than $$\mathrm{F}_2$$
Fluoride is a good oxidising agent

explanation:
Fluorine act as a strong oxidising agent that oxidises water to dioxygen. The reaction is spontaneous and strongly exothermic. Fluorine atoms are itself reduced to fluoride ions.

$$\mathrm{F}_2+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_3 \mathrm{O}^{+}+2 \mathrm{~F}^{-}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2$$

with chlorine, an alternate disproportionation reaction occur rapidly

i.e. $$\mathrm{Cl}_2^0+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \stackrel{(-1)}{\mathrm{HCl}}+\stackrel{(+1)}{\mathrm{HOCl}}$$

content: In the reaction of gold with aquaregia, oxidation state of nitrogen changes from
explanation:
Aqua regia is a mixture of concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl) and concentrated nitric acid (HNO3). It has the capability to dissolve gold, which is insoluble in both acids alone.

Let's consider the reactions involved. The overall balanced chemical equation for the reaction of gold with aqua regia can be represented as:

$$ \text{Au} + 4 \text{HCl} + \text{HNO}_3 \rightarrow \text{HAuCl}_4 + \text{NO} + 2 \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

In this reaction, gold (Au) achieves a +3 oxidation state in the product HAuCl4. The nitric acid (HNO3) acts as an oxidizing agent. To focus on the nitrogen in nitric acid, its oxidation state will change during the reaction.

In nitric acid (HNO3), nitrogen has an oxidation state of +5. In the product NO (nitric oxide), nitrogen has an oxidation state of +2.

Thus, the oxidation state of nitrogen changes from +5 to +2.

The correct option is:

Option C: +5 to +2

content:
For the redox reaction

    $$ \begin{aligned} x \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}+y \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4+ & z \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow \\ & m \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+n \mathrm{CO}_2+p \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} . \end{aligned} $$

    The values of $x, y, m$ and $n$ are
explanation:
The possible balance equation is

    $$ \underset{(x)}{2 \mathrm{MnO}_4^{-}}+\underset{(y)}{5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{O}_4}+\underset{(z)}{6 \mathrm{H}^{+}} \longrightarrow \underset{(m)}{2 \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}} \underset{(n)}{10 \mathrm{CO}_2}+\underset{(p)}{8 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}} $$

    Hence,

    $$ \begin{array}{r} x=2 \\ y=5 \\ m=2 \\ n=10 \end{array} $$

    $\therefore$ (b) is the correct option.

content: $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ acts as an oxidising agent in alkaline medium. When alkaline $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ is treated with KI , iodide ion is oxidised to
explanation:
In an alkaline medium, potassium permanganate ($\mathrm{KMnO}_4$) acts as an oxidizing agent. When it is reacted with potassium iodide ($\mathrm{KI}$), the iodide ions ($\mathrm{I}^-$) are oxidized. The reaction proceeds as follows:

$ 2 \mathrm{KMnO}_4 + \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} + \mathrm{KI} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{MnO}_2 + 2 \mathrm{KOH} + \mathrm{KIO}_3 $

In this reaction, the iodide ions ($\mathrm{I}^-$) are oxidized to iodate ions ($\mathrm{IO}_3^-$).

content: Extraction of chlorine from brine solution is based on
explanation:
The extraction of chlorine from a brine solution is based on the oxidation process. Here's a brief explanation:

In the chlor-alkali process, brine (a concentrated sodium chloride solution) is electrolyzed.

At the positive electrode (anode), chloride ions ($$Cl^-$$) lose electrons (i.e., they are oxidized) to form chlorine gas:

$$2Cl^- \rightarrow Cl_2 + 2e^-$$

This oxidation of chloride ions is the key step in extracting chlorine.

Thus, the correct answer is:

Option A: oxidation.

$\because$ Oxino. of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$changes from $(+1)$ to $(-1)$

$\because$ It is an example of reduction.
